speaker: doctor
text: Good morning, Mr. Thompson. Thanks for coming in today. Let's start by reviewing your current medications and health status.

speaker: doctor
text: You're currently on Lisinopril 20mg once daily — we started that back in February to help manage your blood pressure and protect your kidneys.

speaker: doctor
text: As we discussed at your last visit, your diagnosis is Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3, confirmed by eGFR trending between 35–44 mL/min/1.73m² over the past six months.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I remember. I’ve been trying to watch my salt intake and stay hydrated, but I’ve noticed I’m more tired than usual lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose at our visit on February 4th was 5.4 mmol/L — well within the normal range, so no signs of diabetes at this time.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate was 90 bpm during that same visit — slightly elevated, though not alarming given your reported activity level and stress.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure was 75 mmHg — good, though your systolic was 142, so we’re still aiming for tighter control.

speaker: patient
text: Right — and I did mention fatigue before, but it’s gotten worse over the past two weeks. Sometimes I nap after lunch just to get through the afternoon.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been feeling fatigued — it’s been going on since early February, and it’s not improving even with more sleep.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — fatigue can be multifactorial in CKD: anemia, electrolyte shifts, or even medication side effects. Let’s check your hemoglobin and ferritin today.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. I also noticed my ankles have been a little puffy the last few days — maybe more than usual?

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll assess for volume overload — edema could suggest worsening renal perfusion or fluid retention. I’ll listen to your lungs and check JVP too.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked my blood sugar at home this morning, it was 7.1 mmol/L — higher than that reading from February 4th.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for sharing that — that number is above our target fasting range. Given your CKD and the upward trend, I’d like to repeat HbA1c and consider whether early dysglycemia is contributing to your fatigue.

speaker: patient
text: And the Lisinopril — I read online it can cause tiredness. Could that be it?

speaker: doctor
text: It’s possible — ACE inhibitors like Lisinopril do list fatigue as a less common side effect. Given your persistent symptoms and the new glucose elevation, I am going to switch you to Losartan 50mg daily instead.

speaker: patient
text: Losartan? Okay — will that help with the swelling too?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — it’s also an antihypertensive with renal protective effects, and often better tolerated for fatigue. We’ll monitor your potassium and creatinine closely after the switch.

speaker: patient
text: That sounds good. By the way, the fatigue hasn’t improved since that reading from last time — I’m still struggling most days.

speaker: doctor
text: Understood — let’s treat this holistically: adjust the med, run labs today, and schedule a follow-up in two weeks to review the results and reassess that number from last time.
